description: A DevOps engineer on your team asked you to prepare a **very small, reproducible firewall-configuration demo** that they can paste into a knowledge-base article. All work must stay under the already-created directory /home/user/firewall_config. Your goals (do all of them exactly): 1. Create a file called /home/user/firewall_config/iptables.rules 1.1. The file must be **exactly seven lines long**, in the **exact order** shown below (no extra blank lines, no leading/trailing spaces): ``` *filter :INPUT DROP [0:0] -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.1.100/32 --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT COMMIT ``` (Make sure the file ends with a single newline character.) 2. Record a miniature “change-log” documenting when the four rules were added. • File path: /home/user/firewall_config/changes.log • The log must contain **exactly four lines**, one per rule, in the same order as the rules appear in iptables.rules. • **Format each line precisely** as: ``` YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS – ADDED – ``` where: • The timestamp must be **2024-01-15 14:30:00** for every line (use this fixed value). • <rule> is the literal iptables rule text that begins with “-A INPUT …”. Example of a single finished line (for SSH): ``` 2024-01-15 14:30:00 – ADDED – -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT ``` When you are done, /home/user/firewall_config/changes.log will therefore contain four such lines. 3. After both files are in place, **display the contents of /home/user/firewall_config/iptables.rules to stdout** so that the engineer sees the resulting rule-set in the terminal session. That’s it—no need for real firewall commands; we are only creating the artefact files. If any verification fails, automated tests will flag it, so mind the exact spelling, order, spacing, timestamps and newline requirements above.
truth:
Expected artefacts after the agent finishes:

1. /home/user/firewall_config/iptables.rules  
   (seven lines, final newline):
   *filter\n
   :INPUT DROP [0:0]\n
   -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT\n
   -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT\n
   -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT\n
   -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.1.100/32 --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT\n
   COMMIT\n

2. /home/user/firewall_config/changes.log  
   (four lines, each ending with newline):
   2024-01-15 14:30:00 – ADDED – -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT\n
   2024-01-15 14:30:00 – ADDED – -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT\n
   2024-01-15 14:30:00 – ADDED – -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT\n
   2024-01-15 14:30:00 – ADDED – -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.1.100/32 --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT\n

3. During the terminal session, the agent must output the entire exact content of /home/user/firewall_config/iptables.rules to stdout as a single block (stdout capture is checked but not case-sensitive to path listings).

No additional files should be created inside /home/user/firewall_config or elsewhere.
